verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 1 — The Solitary Self and Cosmic Creation
verses.dev: ॐ आत्मा वा इदमेक एवाग्र आसीन्नान्यत्किंचन मिषत् । स ईक्षत लोकान्नु सृजा इति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: oṃ ātmā vā idameka evāgra āsīnnānyatkiṃcana miṣat | sa īkṣata lokānnu sṛjā iti || 1 ||
verses.trans: In the beginning, all this was verily the Self alone. There was nothing else whatsoever that winked or active. He thought: 'Let Me now project the worlds.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara's commentary on this opening verse is a foundational text for non-dualism. He argues that 'Atman' here cannot refer to any individual soul or material cause, but to the supreme, non-dual Consciousness (*Nirguṇa Brahman*). The phrase 'in the beginning' (*Agre*) refers to the state before the manifestation of name and form. The word 'ekah' (one) explicitly denies any internal parts, and 'eva' (alone) rules out any external material cause (like the Prakriti of Sankhya). The absolute Self 'thought' (*Īkṣata*); this is not a psychological or biological thought, but the spontaneous, unforced turning of absolute awareness toward creation via Maya.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja (as elaborated by later commentators like Raṅgarāmānuja) interprets 'Atman' as the Supreme Lord (*Parameśvara* or *Nārāyaṇa*), who possesses all subtle sentient souls (*Cit*) and insentient matter (*Acit*) as His eternal body in an unmanifest state (*Sūkṣma*). Creation is not an illusion; it is the physical manifestation (*Sthūla*) of what already existed subtly within Him. His cosmic sight (*Īkṣaṇa*) represents the exercise of His absolute, conscious will to diversify His own body for the spiritual evolution of the individual souls.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya asserts that this verse confirms the ultimate independence (*Svatantra*) of Lord Viṣṇu. Before creation, the universe existed as a dependent potential reality (*Paratantra*), but Viṣṇu existed entirely alone as the independent efficient cause. Madhva rejects any idea that the world is an illusion or a modification of God's essence; rather, the Lord uses His independent power to manifest distinct, real material elements and souls, which remain eternally separate from Him.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 1 — The Solitary Self and Cosmic Creation
verses.dev: स इमाँल्लोकानसृजत । अम्भो मरीचीर्मरमापोऽदोऽम्भः परेण दिवं द्यौः प्रतिष्ठाऽन्तरिक्षं मरीचयः पृथिवी मरो या अधस्तात्ता आपः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa imā~llōkānasṛjata | ambhō marīcīrmaramāpō'dō'mbhaḥ pareṇa divaṃ dyauḥ pratiṣṭhā'ntarikṣaṃ marīcayaḥ pṛthivī marō yā adhastāttā āpaḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: He projected these worlds: Ambhas (the cosmic waters above heaven), Marīcis (the rays of light or space), Mara (the earth, where creatures die), and Āpas (the waters underneath the earth). That is Ambhas which is beyond heaven; heaven is its support. The atmosphere is Marīcis. The earth is Mara. The waters below are Āpas.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that these four worlds encompass the entire phenomenal macrocosm. They are built out of name and form, projected like a magician's illusion to serve as the theater of karmic experience. By categorizing the universe into these distinct physical zones, the Upanishad systematically prepares the seeker's mind to look past the external elements and search for the single, unattached Witness that projected them.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The four spheres are real cosmic structures designed by the Lord. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that 'Ambhas' refers to the pristine celestial waters above the material heavens. The description demonstrates the precise spatial order and structural planning of the universe, proving that creation is a real, orderly, and purposeful action of the Divine Architect.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the specific naming of these four distinct worlds reflects their genuine, objective reality and boundaries. They are not mental projections. Each world has its own distinct set of presiding deities who operate under the strict hierarchical control of Viṣṇu, who pervades and rules all four zones without being modified by them.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 1 — The Solitary Self and Cosmic Creation
verses.dev: स ईक्षतेमे नु लोका लोकपालान्नु सृजा इति । सोऽद्भ्य एव पुरुषं समुद्धृत्यामुूर्च्छयत् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa īkṣateme nu lokā lokapālānnu sṛjā iti | so'dbhya eva puruṣaṃ samuddhṛtyāmūrcchayat || 3 ||
verses.trans: He thought: 'Here are the worlds. Let Me now project the guardians of the worlds.' Gathering the Puruṣa (the Cosmic Man, Virāj) from the waters, He shaped him and gave him form.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that after creating the environments (the worlds), the Self projects the macrocosmic structural blueprint—the *Virāj* or *Hiraṇyagarbha* (the Cosmic Man). This Puruṣa is pulled 'from the waters,' meaning out of the unrefined, subtle material elements. Giving him form is like a potter shaping clay. This shows that the cosmic body is a structured instrument designed specifically to manifest consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Puruṣa created here is the macrocosmic soul, Hiraṇyagarbha, who serves as the primary instrument of the Lord's creative will. The Lord draws him out from the primordial waters to act as the secondary creator and overseer of the physical cosmos, highlighting the structured chain of divine administration.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that the 'Puruṣa' formed here is the cosmic form of Caturmukha Brahmā (the four-faced creator god), who is the highest dependent soul (*Jīva*). The Lord extracts and empowers Brahmā from the causal waters to serve as the chief administrator of the universe, reinforcing the eternal hierarchy between God and the highest jīva.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 1 — The Solitary Self and Cosmic Creation
verses.dev: तमभ्यतपत् तस्याभितप्तस्य मुखं निरभिद्यत यथाऽण्डं मुखाद्वाग्वाचोऽग्निः नासिके निरभिद्येतां नासिकाभ्यां प्राणः प्राणाद्वायुः अक्षीणी निरभिद्येतामक्षीभ्यां चक्षुश्चक्षुष आदित्यः कर्णौ निरभिद्येतां कर्णाभ्यां श्रोत्रं श्रोत्राद्दिशः त्वङ्निरभिद्यत त्वचो लोमानि लोमभ्य ओषधिवनस्पतयो हृदयं निरभिद्यत हृदयान्मनो मनसश्चन्द्रमा नाभिर्निरभिद्यत नाभ्या अपानोऽपानान्मृत्युः शिश्नं निरभिद्यत शिश्नाद्रेतो रेतस आपः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: tamabhyatapat tasyābhitaptasya mukhaṃ nirabhidyata yathā'ṇḍaṃ mukhādvāgvācō'gniḥ nāsikē nirabhidyētāṃ nāsikābhyāṃ prāṇaḥ prāṇādvāyuḥ akṣīṇī nirabhidyētāmakṣībhyāṃ cakṣuścakṣuṣa ādityaḥ karṇau nirabhidyētāṃ karṇābhyāṃ śrōtraṃ śrōtrāddiśaḥ tvaṅnirabhidyata tvacō lōmāni lōmabhya ōṣadhivanaspatayō hṛdayaṃ nirabhidyata hṛdayānmanō manasaścandramā nābhirnirabhidyata nābhyā apānō'pānānmṛtyuḥ śiśnaṃ nirabhidyata śiśnādrētō rētasa āpaḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: He brooded over him (with austerity). As he was thus brooded over, his mouth burst open, like an egg. From the mouth proceeded speech, and from speech, Fire. His nostrils burst open; from the nostrils proceeded the vital breath, and from breath, Air. His eyes burst open; from the eyes proceeded sight, and from sight, the Sun. His ears burst open; from the ears proceeded hearing, and from hearing, the Quarters of Space. His skin burst open; from the skin proceeded hairs, and from the hairs, herbs and big trees. His heart burst open; from the heart proceeded the mind, and from the mind, the Moon. His navel burst open; from the navel proceeded the downward breath (Apāna), and from Apāna, Death. His organ of generation burst open; from it proceeded semen, and from semen, Water.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that 'brooding' (*Abhyatapat*) means the exercise of cosmic contemplation. The organic development of the Cosmic Man is described like an egg cracking open. This verse traces the profound structural correlation between the subjective organ (*Adhyātma*), the objective function (*Adhibhūta*), and the cosmic deity (*Adhidaiva*). This proves that the individual micro-organism is a mirrored reflection of the macrocosm, showing they share a single underlying reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this biological differentiation reveals how the cosmic deities are assigned their respective duties within the universal body. The Lord systematically designs the openings of the senses so that the universal administrators (Fire, Sun, Air) can take their places, ensuring the universe operates as a highly coordinated, living divine body.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the hatching of the cosmic organs represents the systematic placement of the presiding deities under the direction of the Supreme Lord. The cosmic deities (like Agni, Sūrya, and Vāyu) do not function independently; they are appointed to these physical sensory gateways to execute the orders of Viṣṇu, who dwells inside them as their inner ruler.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 2 — The Descent of the Deities
verses.dev: ता एता देवताः सृष्टा अस्मिन्महत्यर्णवे प्रापतन् । तमशनापिपासाभ्यामन्ववार्जत् । ता एनमब्रुवन्नायतनं नः प्रजानीहि यस्मिन्प्रतिष्ठिता अन्नमदामेति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tā etā devatāḥ sṛṣṭā asminmahatyarṇavē prāpatan | tamaśanāpipāsābhyāmanvavārjat | tā enamabruvannāyatanaṃ naḥ prajānīhi yasminpratiṣṭhitā annamadāmēti || 1 ||
verses.trans: These deities, thus created, fell into this vast ocean of worldly existence. He subjected the Cosmic Man to hunger and thirst. Then those deities said to Him: 'Find an abode for us wherein we may rest firmly and eat food.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara points out that the 'vast ocean' (*Mahatyarṇave*) is the painful sea of Saṃsāra, driven by ignorance, desire, and action. Hunger and thirst are the root expressions of lack and limitation. The deities themselves, despite being cosmic cosmic cosmic administrators, feel incomplete without a localized body. This demonstrates that all conditioned states—even celestial ones—are bound by limitation, and need a physical vessel to fulfill their experiences.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The cosmic deities find themselves placed in the material universe, experiencing the natural limitations of cosmic service. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that hunger and thirst are created by the Lord as structural impulses to drive activity within creation. The deities request a specific physical vehicle to carry out their divine functions and enjoy their designated offerings.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that the deities do not experience the binding misery of ordinary human hunger and thirst. Instead, their hunger represents a functional need for structural sustenance and a desire to receive sacrificial offerings (*Bhāga*). They ask the Independent Lord to designate fit physical bodies where they can systematically carry out their cosmic duties.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 2 — The Descent of the Deities
verses.dev: ताभ्यो गामानयत्ता अब्रुवन्न वै नोऽयमलमिति । ताभ्योऽश्वमानयत्ता अब्रुवन्न वै नोऽयमलमिति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tābyō gāmānayattā abruvanna vai nō'yamalamiti | tābyō'śvamānayattā abruvanna vai nō'yamalamiti || 2 ||
verses.trans: He brought a cow to them. They said: 'Verily, this is not sufficient for us.' He brought a horse to them. They said: 'Verily, this is not sufficient for us.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara comments that the evolutionary scale of physical bodies is tested here. The cow and horse represent lower animal structures. The deities reject them because these simpler biological organisms lack the complex nervous and intellectual architecture needed to process the full range of cosmic sensory functions and higher conscious expressions.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The animal forms are brought forth to demonstrate the evolutionary gradation of bodies. The cosmic deities find these animal structures inadequate because their physical instruments are not complex enough to house advanced sensory and intellectual operations, which require a higher anatomical frame.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the rejection of the cow and horse forms shows that these biological bodies are designed for limited types of souls. The cosmic administrators require a superior, more sophisticated physical framework to execute advanced cosmic actions and process refined mental offerings.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 2 — The Descent of the Deities
verses.dev: ताभ्यः पुरुषमानयत्ता अब्रुवन् सुकृतं बतेति पुरुषो वाव सुकृतम् । ता अब्रवीद्यथायतनं प्रविशतेति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tābyaḥ puruṣamānayattā abruvan sukṛtaṃ batēti puruṣō vāva sukṛtam | tā abrēvīdyathāyatanaṃ praviśatēti || 3 ||
verses.trans: He brought a human being to them. They exclaimed: 'Ah, well created indeed! A human being is verily a masterpiece of self-creation.' He said to them: 'Enter now into your respective abodes.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The human body is celebrated as 'Sukṛtam' (perfectly created). Shankara notes that the human frame is the supreme physical vehicle because it is uniquely equipped with an intellect (*Buddhi*) capable of both processing empirical knowledge and achieving ultimate liberation (*Mokṣa*). The human body is the ideal micro-cosmic temple where the macrocosmic forces can integrate and operate seamlessly.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The human form is the crown of the Lord's material creation. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the human body is uniquely structured to allow both action (*Karma*) and wisdom (*Jñāna*). The deities praise it because its complex nervous and mental setup provides the perfect structural environment for them to execute their functions.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that 'Sukṛtam' means the human body is designed beautifully to align with moral laws and spiritual progress. It is the only material vessel that allows an individual soul to practice conscious devotion and realize its natural subordination to Viṣṇu. The Lord orders the deities into this form to guide human actions from within.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 2 — The Descent of the Deities
verses.dev: अग्निर्वाग्भूत्वा मुखं प्राविशद्वायुः प्राणो भूत्वा नासिके प्राविशदादित्यश्चक्षुर्भूत्वाऽक्षिणी प्राविशद्दिशः श्रोत्रं भूत्वा कर्णौ प्राविशन्नोषधिवनस्पतयो लोमानि भूत्वा त्वचं प्राविशँश्चन्द्रमा मनो भूत्वा हृदयं प्राविशन्मृत्युरपानो भूत्वा नाभिं प्राविशदापो रेतो भूत्वा शिश्नं प्राविशन् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: agnirvāgbhūtvā mukhaṃ prāviśadvāyuḥ prāṇō bhūtvā nāsikē prāviśadādityaścakṣurbhūtvā'kṣiṇī prāviśaddiśaḥ śrōtraṃ bhūtvā karṇau prāviśannōṣadhivanaspatayō lōmāni bhūtvā tvacaṃ prāviśa~ścandramā manō bhūtvā hṛdayaṃ prāviśanmṛtyurapānō bhūtvā nābhiṃ prāviśadāpō rētō bhūtvā śiśnaṃ prāviśan || 4 ||
verses.trans: Fire became speech and entered the mouth. Air became the vital breath and entered the nostrils. The Sun became sight and entered the eyes. The Quarters of Space became hearing and entered the ears. Herbs and big trees became hairs and entered the skin. The Moon became the mind and entered the heart. Death became the downward breath (Apāna) and entered the navel. Water became semen and entered the organ of generation.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks this process as the profound locking together of the cosmic and individual realms. The universal powers step down into individual functions: cosmic Fire limits itself as human speech; the infinite Sun becomes the organ of sight. This proves that our individual body is not a private possession, but an open, public crossroads where cosmic forces continuously interact under the light of pure Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This structured entry demonstrates that the individual body is a functional microcosm. Every sensory and motor organ is supervised by a specific cosmic administrator appointed by the Supreme Lord. The human body is therefore a living temple where these deities manage physiological systems under the watchful eye of the indwelling soul.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes that the entry of these deities means they take up their administrative posts within the human body. He clarifies that the human senses cannot function by their own power; they require these appointed cosmic deities to act as instruments, while the deities themselves remain completely dependent on the supreme inner controller, Viṣṇu.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 2 — The Descent of the Deities
verses.dev: तमशनापिपासे अब्रूतामावाभ्यामभिप्रजानीहीति ते अब्रवीदेतास्वेव वां देवतासु भज्याम्येतासु भागिन्यौ करोमीति । तस्माद्यस्यै कस्यै च देवतायै हविर्गृह्यते भागिन्यावेव अस्यामशनापिपासे भवतः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tamaśanāpipāsē abrūtāmāvābhyāmabhiprajānīhīti tē abravīdētāsvēva vāṃ dēvatāsu bhajyāmyētāsu bhāginyau karōmīti | tasmādyasyai kasyai ca dēvatāyai havirgṛhyatē bhāginyāvēva asyāmaśanāpipāsē bhavataḥ || 5 ||
verses.trans: Hunger and thirst said to Him: 'For us two also find an abode.' He said to them: 'I assign you a place in these very deities; I make you co-sharers with them.' Therefore, to whatever deity an offering is made, hunger and thirst become co-sharers in it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that hunger and thirst are not individual entities, but universal structural conditions (*Vṛttis*) of deficiency. By placing them inside the deities, the text demonstrates that no sensory or mental organ can function without consuming energy. Every act of perception or thought consumes something, showing that the entire empirical world is caught in a cycle of constant hunger, dependency, and consumption.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Hunger and thirst are woven by the Lord into the fabric of all physical functions. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that because hunger and thirst share in every deity's offering, all biological and cosmic processes are driven by an inherent need for nutritional and energetic renewal, showing how the Lord perfectly balances the ecosystem.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that hunger and thirst are functional forces controlled by specific subordinate deities. By making them co-sharers in all offerings, the Lord establishes a systematic rule: every physical and cosmic action requires an expenditure of energy, ensuring that all created beings recognize their incomplete, dependent status.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: सोऽपामीक्षतान्नं नु सृजा इति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: so'pāmīkṣatānnaṃ nu sṛjā iti || 1 ||
verses.trans: He (the Supreme Self) thought: 'Here are the worlds and their guardians. Let Me now project food for them.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara states that after the creation of the cosmic theater (worlds) and the actors (deities and the human vehicle), the creation of food is necessary to sustain them. This highlights that all embodied life is structurally dependent on external nourishment, pointing to its finite and conditioned nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the Lord, out of His infinite compassion, creates food to preserve the structural integrity of the living universe. This emphasizes that the material world is designed purposefully by the Divine to support the evolutionary journey of individual souls.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that this verse demonstrates the absolute sovereignty of Viṣṇu. The created deities and living beings have no independent power to sustain themselves; they depend entirely on the food created and provided by the Lord.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: सोऽपोऽभ्यतपत्ताभ्योऽभितप्ताभ्यो मूर्तिरजायत । या वै सा मूर्तिरजायतान्नं वै तत् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: so'po'bhyatapattābhyo'bhitaptābhyo mūrtirajāyata | yā vai sā mūrtirajāyatānnaṃ vai tat || 2 ||
verses.trans: He brooded over the waters (the material elements). From the waters thus brooded over, a solid form was born. That solid form which was produced is verily food.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies that 'waters' here stands for all the gross elements combined. The 'solid form' (*Mūrti*) represents matter in a consumable, structured state. Matter is concentrated energy, brought forth to serve as food for the sensory organs.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The solid form is a real, physical modification of the elements under the direction of the Lord. The school asserts that food is an auspicious, divine gift meant to maintain the body so it can be used for spiritual duties.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the manifestation of matter into a concrete, edible form is an expression of the Lord’s unconditioned power to manipulate the elements, showing that all material wealth is owned and governed by Him.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तदेतदभिसृष्टं पराङ्त्यजिघांसत्तद्वाचाऽजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोद्वाचा ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनद्वाचाऽग्रहैष्यदभिव्याहृत्य हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tadetadabhisṛṣṭaṃ parāṅtyajighāṃsattadvācā'jighṛक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोद्वाचा ग्रहीतुम् | sa yaddhainadvācā'grahaiṣyadabhivyāhṛtya haivānnamatrapsyat || 3 ||
verses.trans: This food, thus projected, sought to flee away. He (the human being) tried to grasp it with speech, but he could not grasp it with speech. If he had grasped it with speech, then by merely uttering the name of food, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides a brilliant psychological insight here. The fleeing of food represents the natural instability of matter. The attempt of the individual organs to grasp it one by one reveals that no single sensory or motor faculty can function outside its specific biological domain. Speech can only name food; it cannot physically capture or assimilate it.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The failure of the single organs shows that the human body is a highly specialized piece of engineering designed by the Lord. Each organ has a strictly limited jurisdiction, preventing any single faculty from dominating the biological system.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets this sequence as proof of the inherent limitation and helplessness of the human organs. The senses cannot perform any action outside what has been explicitly decreed for them by the Supreme Lord, highlighting their total dependency.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तत्प्राणेनाजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोत्प्राणेन ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनत्प्राणेनाग्रहैष्यदभिप्राय्य हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: tatprāṇenājighṛkṣattannāśaknotprāṇena grahītum | sa yaddhainatprāṇenāgrahaiṣyadabhiprāyya haivānnamatrapsyat || 4 ||
verses.trans: He tried to grasp it with the breath (nostrils), but he could not grasp it with the breath. If he had grasped it with the breath, then by merely smelling food, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that smelling food provides cognitive recognition of its presence but fails to deliver the actual nourishment needed to sustain life. The organs remain unfulfilled.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The sense of smell is an auxiliary instrument. The Lord has structured the body such that smelling acts as an indicator of food quality, but not as the mechanical consumer of it.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes that the specific deities ruling the sense of smell are powerless to extract nutritional value from food, as that function belongs to a separate divine office within the anatomy.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तच्चक्षुषाऽजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोच्चक्षुषा ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनच्चक्षुषाऽग्रहैष्यद्दृष्ट्वा हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: taccakṣuṣā'jighṛkṣattannāśaknoccakṣuṣa grahītum | sa yaddhainaccakṣuṣā'grahaiṣyaddṛṣṭvā haivānnamatrapsyat || 5 ||
verses.trans: He tried to grasp it with the eye (sight), but he could not grasp it with the eye. If he had grasped it with the eye, then by merely seeing food, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The eye can perceive the form and color of food, but sight cannot absorb material substance. Shankara uses this to show that intellectual or sensory observation of an object does not equal absolute possession.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Sight is designed to locate food in space. The Lord denied the eyes the capacity to consume matter, ensuring that the distinct organs must work together in a coordinated ecosystem.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The deity of the sun (*Sūrya*), presiding over the eye, is shown to have limited power. This reinforces the core Dvaita doctrine that all intermediate cosmic powers have strict operational boundaries.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तच्छ्रोत्रेणाजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोच्छ्रोत्रेण ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनच्छ्रोत्रेणाग्रहैष्यच्छ्रुत्वा हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: tacchrotreṇājighṛkṣattannāśaknocchrotreṇa grahītum | sa yaddhainacchrotreṇāgrahaiṣyacchrutvā haivānnamatrapsyat || 6 ||
verses.trans: He tried to grasp it with the ear (hearing), but he could not grasp it with the ear. If he had grasped it with the ear, then by merely hearing about food, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Hearing the sound of cooking or the description of a feast cannot alleviate hunger. Shankara emphasizes that theoretical or audible knowledge of truth is completely different from direct, practical realization.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The ear registers sonic vibrations. The Lord structured this sense to gather environmental information, not to serve as an entry point for material mass.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that this underscores the specific, unchanging laws of nature established by God. An organ cannot trespass onto the functional territory of another.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तत्त्वचाऽजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोत्त्वचा ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनत्त्वचाऽग्रहैष्यत्स्पृष्ट्वा हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: tattvacā'jighṛkṣattannāśaknottvacā grahītum | sa yaddhainattvacā'grahaiṣyatspṛṣṭvā haivānnamatrapsyat || 7 ||
verses.trans: He tried to grasp it with the skin (touch), but he could not grasp it with the skin. If he had grasped it with the skin, then by merely touching food, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Tactile contact registers texture and temperature, but it cannot feed the body. Shankara shows that external physical contact with the world is superficial and leaves the inner self starved.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Touch provides physical awareness of matter. The Lord built this mechanism for protection and handling, leaving the consumption of food to a deeper internal process.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The deities of touch cannot act as sustainers of life. Madhva views this as an illustration of how every minor administrative power in the cosmos has a fixed, non-negotiable duty.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तन्मनसाऽजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोन्मनसा ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनन्मनसाऽग्रहैष्यद्ध्यात्वा हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: tanmanasā'jighṛkṣattannāśaknonmanasa grahītum | sa yaddhainanmanasā'grahaiṣyaddhyātvā haivānnamatrapsyat || 8 ||
verses.trans: He tried to grasp it with the mind, but he could not grasp it with the mind. If he had grasped it with the mind, then by merely thinking of food, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Mental imagination of food cannot satisfy physical hunger. Shankara warns that mere intellectual speculation or conceptual thinking about Brahman will never replace direct non-dual experience (*Aparokṣa-Anubhūti*).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The mind coordinates desires but cannot materialize physical nutrition. This shows that human thought is dependent on a real, external material reality created by the Lord; it cannot create its own sustenance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the human mind is highly limited. It cannot sustain the body by mere thought, demonstrating that the individual is completely dependent on physical laws engineered by Viṣṇu.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तच्छिश्नेनाजिघृक्षत्तन्नाशक्नोच्छिश्नेन ग्रहीतुम् । स यद्धैनच्छिश्नेनाग्रहैष्यद्विसृज्य हैवान्नमत्रप्स्यत् ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: tacchiśnenājighṛkṣattannāśaknocchiśnena grahītum | sa yaddhainacchiśnenāgrahaiṣyadvisṛjya haivānnamatrapsyat || 9 ||
verses.trans: He tried to grasp it with the generative organ, but he could not grasp it with that organ. If he had grasped it with the generative organ, then by merely emitting semen, one would have been satisfied.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that the reproductive system deals with the output of life and cellular energy, not the input of basic physical energy. It cannot serve as the entry gate for nutrition.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The reproductive organ has a specific procreative purpose designed by the Creator. It cannot perform the work of nutritional assimilation, which requires its own dedicated system.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: This verse further demonstrates the strict compartmentalization of bodily functions. Madhva notes that every physical faculty is locked into its own organic purpose by divine decree.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तदपानेनाजिघृक्षत्तदावयत् सैषोऽन्नस्य ग्रहो यद्वायुरन्नायुर्वा एष यद्वायुः ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast: tadapānenājighṛkṣattadāvayat saiṣo'nnasya graho yadvāyurannāyurvā eṣa yadvāyuḥ || 10 ||
verses.trans: He then tried to grasp it with the digestive breath (Apāna). He consumed it. This grasper of food is verily Apāna. This vital air (Vāyu) is that which lives on food.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Finally, success is achieved through *Apāna*. Shankara clarifies that in this context, Apāna refers to the digestive breath or the metabolic system centered in the stomach and digestive tract. It is the physiological faculty that actually digests and absorbs food. Because it digests food to generate life-force, this breath is called *Annāyu*—the life-span that depends entirely on food.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord designed the Apāna function to be the direct consumer of food. Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this digestive process breaks down physical matter into energy, transforming external food into the internal fuel needed to keep the soul's physical vehicle alive.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that *Vāyu* (the cosmic wind deity) operates within the body as the vital lifeforce (*Mukhya-Prāṇa*). Its sub-division, Apāna, acts under the direct command of Viṣṇu to process physical food, proving that even our primary life-support systems are driven by divine agency.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: स ईक्षत कथं नु इदं मदृते स्यादिति स ईक्षत कतरेण प्रपद्या इति । स ईक्षत यदि वाचाऽभिव्याहृतं यदि प्राणेनाभिप्राणितं यदि चक्षुषा दृष्टं यदि श्रोत्रेण श्रुतं यदि त्वचा स्पृष्टं यदि मनसा ध्यातं यद्यपानेनाभ्यपानितं यदि शिश्नेन विसृष्टमथ कोऽहमिति ॥ ११ ॥
verses.iast: sa īkṣata kathaṃ nu idaṃ madṛte syāditi sa īkṣata katareṇa prapadyā iti | sa īkṣata yadi vācā'bhivyāhṛtaṃ yadi prāṇenābhiprāṇitaṃ yadi cakṣuṣā dṛṣṭaṃ yadi śrotreṇa śrutaṃ yadi tvacā spṛṣṭaṃ yadi manasā dhyātaṃ yadyapānenābhyapānitaṃ yadi śiśnena visṛṣṭamatha ko'hamiti || 11 ||
verses.trans: He (the Supreme Self) thought: 'How can all this exist without Me?' He considered: 'By which door shall I enter it?' He thought: 'If speaking is done by speech, breathing by breath, seeing by the eye, hearing by the ear, touching by the skin, thinking by the mind, eating by the digestive breath, and emitting by the generative organ—then who or what am I?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A massive philosophical peak. Shankara points out that a house built for a king is completely useless unless the king actually moves in to occupy it. The physical body and its sensory systems are merely instruments. If they could function independently to generate life and meaning, the body would have no need for a higher soul. The question 'Who am I?' (*Atha ko'ham*) is the core of Self-inquiry. It demonstrates that the absolute Self is not any collection of senses or functions, but the independent Witness (*Sākṣī*) that gives them all their light and purpose.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord looks at the human vehicle and decides to enter it to become its ultimate inner soul (*Antaryāmī*). The body and individual soul cannot function for a single moment without His direct presence. His entry is an act of pure grace, turning the human body into a holy temple for His own habitation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that this verse proves that the individual soul and its senses are entirely inert and helpless on their own. The Supreme Lord Viṣṇu must enter the body to act as the primary catalyst and source of consciousness. Without the Lord's presence, the body would collapse like an engine without fuel.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: स एतमेव सीमानं विदार्यैतया द्वारा प्रापद्यत । सैषा द्वार्विधृतिर्नाम तदेतन्नान्दनम् । तस्य त्रय आवसथास्त्रयः स्वप्ना अयमावसथोऽयमावसथोऽयमावसथ इति ॥ १२ ॥
verses.iast: sa etameva sīmānaṃ vidāryaitayā dvārā prāpadyata | saiṣā dvārvidṛtirnāma tadetannāndanam | tasya traya āvasathāstrayaḥ svapnā ayamāvasatho'yamāvasatho'yamāvasatha iti || 12 ||
verses.trans: Splitting open the crown of the head (the sagittal suture), He entered by that door. This door is named Vidṛti (the cleft door). This is the place of absolute bliss (Nandana). He has three abodes in the body, and three states of sleep (dream-like relative consciousness), which are: this abode (the right eye during waking), this abode (the mind during dreaming), and this abode (the space of the heart during deep sleep).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that *Vidṛti* is the highest energetic entry point, located at the crown of the skull (the *Brahmarandhra*). This door is called *Nāndanam* because it leads directly to the experience of absolute bliss when opened via yoga and non-dual realization. The 'three abodes' are the physiological centers for the three states of consciousness: the right eye during waking (*Jāgrat*), the inner mind during dreaming (*Svapna*), and the core of the heart during deep sleep (*Suṣupti*). Shankara drops a profound truth bomb: the Upanishad calls all three states—including the waking state—'sleep' or 'dreams' (*Svapnāḥ*) because they are all products of ignorance, masking the true non-dual reality of the Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The crown of the head is the sacred pathway used by the Lord to place His presence inside the human frame, and it is also the exit route (*Arcirādi-mārga*) through which the liberated soul ascends to the eternal realm (*Vaikuṇṭha*). The three abodes are the designated physiological stations where the individual soul, accompanied by the inner Lord, experiences different levels of material reality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the entry through the crown as the specific method by which the Lord establishes His supreme control over the nervous system. The three abodes are the centers where the Lord manifests distinct forms of Himself (Viśva in waking, Taijasa in dreaming, and Prājña in deep sleep) to govern the individual soul's experiences throughout day and night.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: स जातो भूतान्यभिव्यैख्यत् किमिहान्यं वावदिषदिति । स एतमेव पुरुषं ब्रह्म तततममपश्यदिदमदर्शमिति ॥ १३ ॥
verses.iast: sa jāto bhūtānyabhivyaikhyat kimihānyaṃ vāvadiṣaditi | sa etameva puruṣaṃ brahma tatatamamapaśyadidamadarśamiti || 13 ||
verses.trans: Having entered the body, He looked around at all created beings. He wondered: 'What else here is there for Me to name or discover as separate from Me?' He saw this very Puruṣa as the all-pervading Brahman. He exclaimed: 'I have seen It! (Idam-adarśam)'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The peak of cosmic awakening. Shankara explains that once Consciousness enters the human vehicle and wakes up to its true nature, the illusion of separation dissolves completely. The realized soul looks at the universe and sees absolutely nothing other than itself. It realizes that the individual body is actually the all-pervading *Brahman* (*Brahma tatatamam*). The exclamation 'I have seen It!' marks the immediate, direct shift from individual ego to universal, non-dual awareness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The soul, looking through its refined intellect, sees that the entire universe is filled and sustained by the supreme Puruṣa. It realizes that nothing can exist independently of the Lord, and breaks into joyful praise of His all-pervasive presence and glory.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the realized soul looks at creation and sees the supreme, all-pervading influence of Brahman ruling over every atom. The statement 'I have seen It' denotes the highest stage of direct perception (*Aparokṣa-jñāna*) of the Lord's form, which leads to final release by His grace.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Khaṇḍa 3 — Creation of Food and Entry of the Self
verses.dev: तस्मादिदन्द्रो नामेद्रो ह वै नाम । तमिदन्द्रं सन्तमिन्द्र इत्याचक्षते परोक्षेण । परोक्षप्रिया इव हि देवाः परोक्षप्रिया इव हि देवाः ॥ १४ ॥
verses.iast: tasmādidandro nāmedro ha vai nāma | tamidandraṃ santamindra ityācakṣate parōkṣēṇa | parōkṣapriyā iva hi dēvāḥ parōkṣapriyā iva hi dēvāḥ || 14 ||
verses.trans: Therefore, He is called Idandra. 'Idandra' is indeed His secret name. Though He is Idandra, He is called indirectly as Indra. For the gods love that which is hidden and cryptic; they love that which is indirect.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara derives *Idandra* from *Idam-dra* ('He who sees this immediate, direct reality'). The absolute Self is called 'Idandra' because It is the direct Witness of everything. To protect this sacred truth from superficial minds, it is cloaked in the mythic name *Indra*. The phrase 'the gods love the cryptic' (*Parokṣapriyā iva hi devāḥ*) means that deep spiritual truths are naturally hidden behind symbols and metaphors, requiring deep inquiry to be uncovered.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The name Indra is applied here to the Supreme Ruler who directly sees and controls all creation. The preference for indirect or esoteric names reflects the profound mystery of the Divine Nature, which cannot be captured by ordinary, superficial language.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that *Idandra* denotes the Lord as the unique, direct perceiver of the absolute truth (*Idam-dra*). The name *Indra* belongs primarily to the Supreme Lord Viṣṇu as the possessor of ultimate cosmic majesty (*Aiśvarya*), and only secondarily to the chief of the devas.

verses.sec: Chapter II — The Three Births of the Soul
verses.dev: पुरुषे ह वा अयमादितो गर्भो भवति यदेतद्रेतः । तदेतत्सर्वेभ्योऽङ्गेभ्यस्तेजः सम्भूतमात्मन्येवात्मानं बिभर्ति तद्यदा स्त्रियां सिञ्चत्यथैनज्जनयति तदस्य प्रथमं जन्म ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: puruṣe ha va ayamādito garbho bhavati yadetadretaḥ | tadetatsarvebhyo'ṅgebhyostejaḥ sambhūtamātmanyevātmānaṃ bibharti tadyadā striyāṃ siñcatyathainajjanayati tadasya prathamaṃ janma || 1 ||
verses.trans: In a man, indeed, this soul becomes at first a fetus in the form of semen. This semen is the concentrated essence of energy gathered from all the limbs of the body. He carries this self within his own self. When he pours this into a woman, he causes it to be born. This is the soul's first birth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that Chapter 2 shifts to a worldly analysis to shock the seeker into detachment (*Vairāgya*). By tracing the biological and messy origins of the physical body, the Upanishad proves that the physical vehicle is a temporary, changing container. The soul's 'first birth' is its entry into the maternal womb via paternal seed, showing its complete subordination to karmic laws before it can even breathe on its own.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views this verse as proof of the real journey of the individual soul (*Jīva*). The jīva, bound by past karma, is guided by the inner Lord into physical materials to obtain a body. The biological process is a real, divine mechanism designed to give the soul a new field of action for spiritual refinement.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the physical collection of energy represents the dependent status of the individual. The soul does not choose its birth parameters; the entire biological transition is structured and driven by the supreme administrative laws of Viṣṇu, who matches the soul to its biological vessel based on past actions.

verses.sec: Chapter II — The Three Births of the Soul
verses.dev: तद्धि स्त्रिया आत्मभूयं गच्छति यथा स्वमङ्गं तथा । तस्मादेनां न हिनस्ति सास्यैतमात्मानमत्र गतं भावयति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: taddhi striyā ātmabhūyaṃ gacchati yathā svamaṅgaṃ tathā | tasmādenāṃ na hinasti sāsyaitamātmānamatra gataṃ bhāvayati || 2 ||
verses.trans: It becomes one with the body of the woman, just like her own limbs. Therefore, it does not cause her injury. She nurtures this self of his that has entered into her.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the physical integration within the womb mimics the absolute non-dual unity of existence, but on a gross, biological level. This mutual dependence shows how the material world simulates unity through biological ties, keeping the individual soul attached to relative life.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The biological harmony between mother and child is an example of the organic unity found across the universe. Just as the child is an extension of the mother's body, the entire cosmos—including all mothers and children—is an extension of the cosmic body of the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the safe preservation of the child within the mother is an example of the localized protection provided by the inner deities under Viṣṇu's command, ensuring that the natural laws of biology function safely.

verses.sec: Chapter II — The Three Births of the Soul
verses.dev: सा भावयित्री भावयितव्या भवति । तं स्त्री गर्भं बिभर्ति । सोऽग्र एव कुमारं जन्मनोऽग्रेऽधिभावयति । स यत्कुमारं जन्मनोऽग्रेऽधिभावयत्यात्मानमेव तद्भावयत्येषां लोकानां सन्तत्या । एवं सन्तता हीमे लोकास्तदस्य द्वितीयं जन्म ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sā bhāvayitrī bhāvayitavyā bhavati | taṃ strī garbhaṃ bibharti | so'gra eva kumāraṃ janmano'gre'dhibhāvayati | sa yatkumāraṃ janmano'gre'dhibhāvayatiyātmānameva tadbhāvayatyeṣāṃ lokānāṃ santatyā | evaṃ santatā hīme lokāstadasya dvitīyaṃ janma || 3 ||
verses.trans: She, who is the nurturer, must herself be nurtured. The woman carries the fetus. Right after birth, the father nurtures the child. By nurturing the child from birth, he actually nurtures his own self, ensuring the continuation of the worlds. For thus are these worlds continued. This is the soul's second birth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The physical birth into the external world is the 'second birth.' Shankara emphasizes that worldly continuation (*Santatyā*) is driven by the householder's actions, which are bound by worldly duties (*Samsāra*). The father sees his reflection in the child, which is a psychological projection of the ego seeking to bypass mortality through family lineage.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The continuation of family life is treated as a sacred duty within the divine order (*Dharma*). The father protects the child because the child is a gift from the Lord, meant to carry forward the traditions of worship and service.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the generational chain is a real, functional system created by the Lord to ensure that the material world remains populated with living beings who can work out their karma and pursue salvation.

verses.sec: Chapter II — The Three Births of the Soul
verses.dev: सोऽस्यायमात्मा पुण्येभ्यः कर्मभ्यः प्रतिधीयते । अथास्यायमितर आत्मा कृतकृत्यो वयोगतः प्रैति । स इतः प्रयन्नेव पुनर्जायते तदस्य तृतीयं जन्म ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: so'syāyamātmā puṇyebhyaḥ karmabhyaḥ pratidhīyate | athāsyāyamitara ātmā kṛtakṛtyo vayōgataḥ praiti | sa itaḥ prayanneva punarjāyate tadasya tṛtīyaṃ janma || 4 ||
verses.trans: This child, who is the father's self, is made his substitute for the performance of virtuous deeds. Then the other self of his (the father), having fulfilled his duties and reached old age, departs from this world. Departing from this world, he is born again elsewhere. This is the soul's third birth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The 'third birth' occurs after death, when the soul departs the body and is reincarnated into a new physical frame based on its remaining subconscious impressions (*Vāsanās*). Shankara drops a heavy warning here: this endless cycle of three births is a wheel of suffering for anyone who remains ignorant of their true, non-dual nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Reincarnation is the journey of the finite soul through different material costumes. Raṅgarāmānuja points out that when the father's body wears out, the soul drops it and receives a new body from the Lord, ensuring absolute justice according to the soul's moral record.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The soul's transition to a new life is handled by the laws of karma under the strict supervision of Viṣṇu. Madhva emphasizes that the soul cannot choose its next destination or escape this chain without achieving direct devotion and grace.

verses.sec: Chapter II — The Three Births of the Soul
verses.dev: तदुक्तमृषिणा । गर्भे नु सन्नन्वेषामवेदमहं देवानां जनिमानि विश्वा शतं मा पुर आयसीररक्षन्नध श्येनो जवसा निरदीयमिति । गर्भ एवैतच्छयानो वामदेव एवमुवाच ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: taduktamṛṣiṇā | garbhe nu sannanveṣāmavedamahaṃ devānāṃ janimāni viśvā śataṃ mā pura āyasīraraxannadha śyeno javasā niradīyamiti | garbha evaicchayāno vāmadeva evamuvāca || 5 ||
verses.trans: Regarding this, the Rishi Vāmadeva said: 'While still resting in the womb, I discovered all the births of the gods. A hundred iron citadels held me down, but with the speed of a hawk, I broke through them all.' Sage Vāmadeva spoke this verse while still lying inside his mother's womb.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A glorious breakthrough in the text. Shankara explains that Sage Vāmadeva attained total non-dual realization while still inside the womb because of spiritual practices from his past lives. The 'hundred iron citadels' represent the deep-seated illusions of bodily identification and ignorance that lock the soul in captivity. By realizing 'I am Brahman,' Vāmadeva shattered these mental walls instantly, escaping the cycle of transmigration like a swift hawk breaking out of a cage.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Vāmadeva's enlightenment in the womb shows that the Lord can bestow direct spiritual wisdom on a soul at any moment, regardless of external physical conditions. The iron citadels are the bindings of past karma, which are dissolved the moment the soul achieves complete surrender to the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that Vāmadeva was a highly advanced soul who possessed exceptional spiritual clarity from birth. His realization allowed him to see the dependency of all lesser gods on the Supreme Being, and his swift escape like a hawk represents the liberation granted by the grace of Viṣṇu.

verses.sec: Chapter II — The Three Births of the Soul
verses.dev: स एवं विद्वानस्माच्छरीरभेदादूर्ध्व उत्क्रम्यामुष्मिन्स्वर्गे लोके सर्वान्कामानाप्त्वाऽमृतः समभवत्समभवत् ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: sa evaṃ vidvānasmāccharīrabhedādūrdhva utkramyāmuṣminsvarge loke sarvānkāmānāptvā'mṛtaḥ samabhavatsamabhavat || 6 ||
verses.trans: Knowing this, he soared upward after the destruction of the physical body. Ascending into the supreme celestial state of pure Consciousness, he fulfilled all desires and became immortal—verily, he became immortal.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies that 'celestial state' (*Svarge loke*) here does not mean a physical heaven with sensory pleasures. For a non-dualist, it means entering the supreme space of absolute Brahman. 'Fulfilling all desires' means realizing that there is nothing outside of oneself to desire, resulting in absolute contentment and immortality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The liberated soul ascends through the solar path to the eternal realm of the Lord. Reaching this state means the soul is freed from material suffering and enjoys endless bliss in union with God, fulfilling its natural capacity for love.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Immortality is the soul entering *Mokṣa*, the real spiritual paradise where it is freed from rebirth. Madhva notes that the repetition 'he became immortal' emphasizes the permanent, unchanging nature of this free state, where the soul rests under the eternal protection of Sri Hari.

verses.sec: Chapter III — Prajñānam Brahma (The Mahāvākya)
verses.dev: ॐ कोऽयमात्मेति वयमुपास्महे कतरः स आत्मा । येन वा पश्यति येन वा शृणोति येन वा गन्धान्जिघ्रति येन वा वाचं व्याकरोति येन वा स्वादु चास्वादु च विजानाति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: oṃ ko'yamātmeti vayamupāsmahe kataraḥ sa ātmā | yena vā paśyati yena vā śṛṇoti yena vā gandhānjighrati yena vā vācaṃ vyākaroti yena vā svādu cāsvādu ca vijānāti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Who is this Self whom we meditate upon? Which of the two is that Self? Is It that by which one sees form, or by which one hears sound, or by which one smells fragrance, or by which one utters speech, or by which one distinguishes what is tasty from what is tasteless?

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The start of the great psychological analysis in Chapter 3. Shankara states that seekers are asking: Is the Self the physical instruments of perception, or is It the silent power that works through them? The senses are merely windows; they cannot see or hear by themselves. The true Self is the unattached, silent Witness behind the windows, making all sensory experience possible.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The inquiry seeks to identify the true master of the human body. The school notes that while the senses gather information, they require an intelligent director. This ruler is the individual soul, which itself is sustained and guided from within by the Supreme Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that this verse shows that the human body is an organized instrument that cannot run itself. The senses are minor tools managed by inner deities, and the whole system is dependent on the Supreme Will to process life.

verses.sec: Chapter III — Prajñānam Brahma (The Mahāvākya)
verses.dev: यदेतद्धृदयं मनश्चैतत् । संज्ञानमाज्ञानं विज्ञानं प्रज्ञानं मेधा दृष्टिर्धृतिर्मतिर्मनीषा जूतिः स्मृतिः सङ्कल्पः क्रतुरसुः कामो वश इति । सर्वाण्येवैतानी प्रज्ञानस्य नामधेयानि भवन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: yadetaddhṛdayaṃ manaścaitat | saṃjñānamājñānaṃ vijñānaṃ prajñānaṃ medhā dṛṣṭirdhṛtirmatiiramanīṣā jūtiḥ smṛtiḥ saṅkalpaḥ kraturasuḥ kāmo vaśa iti | sarvāṇyevaitānī prajñānasya nāmadheyāni bhavanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: That which is known as the heart and the mind—intellect, specialized knowledge, discrimination, wisdom, memory, resolution, purpose, life-force, desire, and self-control—all these are merely different names and operational functions of pure Consciousness (Prajñāna).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A foundational verse for Advaita psychology. Shankara explains that all mental states—whether logical thoughts, deep memories, emotional desires, or basic willpower—are not independent elements. They are temporary waves appearing on the ocean of pure Consciousness (*Prajñāna*). The mind is inert by itself; it appears conscious only because it reflects the light of this underlying spiritual awareness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The diverse mental faculties are the real capacities of the individual soul's consciousness (*Dharmabhūta-jñāna*). This conscious power expands or contracts based on karma, but its ultimate source and stabilizer is the Supreme Lord, who grants clarity to the mind.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets these sixteen mental states as specific functions managed by subordinate deities inside the mind. These deities operate in a strict, organized structure under the absolute control of Viṣṇu, who is the independent source of all intelligence.

verses.sec: Chapter III — Prajñānam Brahma (The Mahāvākya)
verses.dev: एष ब्रह्मा एष इन्द्र एष प्रजापतिरेते सर्वे देवा इमानि च पञ्चमहाभूतानि पृथिवी वायुराकाश आपो ज्योतींषीत्येतानीमानि च क्षुद्रमिश्राणीव । बीजानीतराणि चेतराणि च अण्डजानि च जारुजानि च स्वेदजानि चोद्भिज्जानि च अश्वा गावः पुरुषा हस्तिनो यत्किञ्चेदं प्राणि जङ्गमं च पतत्रि च यच्च स्थावरम् । सर्वं तत्प्रज्ञानेत्रं प्रज्ञाने प्रतिष्ठितं प्रज्ञानेत्रो लोकः प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठा प्रज्ञानं ब्रह्म ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: eṣa brahmā eṣa indra eṣa prajāpatirete sarve devā imāni ca pañcamahābhūtāni pṛthivī vāyurākāśa āpo jyotīṃṣītyetānīmāni ca kṣudramiśrāṇīva | bījānītarāṇi cetarāṇi ca aṇḍajāni ca jārujāni ca svedajāni codbhijjāni ca aśvā gāvaḥ puruṣā hastino yatkiñcedaṃ prāṇi jaṅgamaṃ ca patatri ca yacca sthāvaram | sarvaṃ tatprajñānetraṃ prajñāne pratiṣṭhitaṃ prajñānetro lokaḥ prajñā pratiṣṭhā prajñānaṃ brahma || 3 ||
verses.trans: He is Brahmā, He is Indra, He is Prajāpati; He is all these gods; He is the five gross elements—Earth, Air, Space, Water, and Light. He is all creatures born from eggs, from wombs, from sweat, and from sprouts; horses, cattle, humans, and elephants—everything that breathes, walks, flies, or remains stationary. All this is guided by Consciousness, and established in Consciousness. The universe is moved by Consciousness. Consciousness is the ultimate foundation. **Consciousness is Brahman (Prajñānaṃ Brahma)**.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The location of the magnificent **Rigvedic Mahāvākya: Prajñānaṃ Brahma**. Shankara’s commentary here is the crown jewel of non-dualism. He explains that everything in existence—from the highest creator god down to a blade of grass, including the physical elements—is nothing but *Prajñāna* (pure Consciousness). The universe is 'guided by Consciousness' (*Prajñā-netram*) and 'established in Consciousness' (*Prajñāne pratiṣṭhitam*). This means the material world has no independent existence outside of awareness. Duality is completely dissolved: the subject, the object, and the act of seeing are all revealed to be variations of a single, non-dual, infinite Reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school interprets this supreme proclamation through the lens of identity with qualifying attributes (*Viśiṣṭādvaita*). Consciousness (*Prajñāna*) is the defining attribute of the Supreme Lord, who is the ultimate source of all wisdom. The statement means that everything in the universe is guided and sustained by the conscious will of Brahman. The world is the body, and the all-knowing Lord is the inner Soul, making the universe completely dependent on Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya strongly rejects any non-dual interpretation that merges the world into God. He explains that *Prajñāna* means the Independent, All-Knowing Intellect of Lord Viṣṇu. The phrase 'Consciousness is Brahman' means that the Supreme Being is pure, infinite Wisdom personified. The statement that everything is established in Consciousness means the entire universe is completely dependent on His supreme knowledge and sovereign control to exist and function.

verses.sec: Chapter III — Prajñānam Brahma (The Mahāvākya)
verses.dev: स एतेन प्रज्ञानेनात्मनास्माल्लोकादुत्क्रम्यामुष्मिन्स्वर्गे लोके सर्वान्कामानाप्त्वाऽमृतः समभवत्समभवत् । ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa etena prajñānenātmanāsmālloakādutkramyāmuṣminsvarge loke sarvānkāmānāptvā'mṛtaḥ samabhavatsamabhavat | oṃ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: By means of this conscious Self, Sage Vāmadeva ascended upward from this world. Entering that supreme, spiritual realm of absolute Bliss, he fulfilled all desires and became immortal—verily, he became immortal. Om Peace, Peace, Peace.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The perfect conclusion to the Upanishad. Shankara notes that anyone who follows Vāmadeva's path of self-inquiry and realizes the Mahāvākya *Prajñānaṃ Brahma* will achieve the exact same freedom. Liberation is not a physical journey to a distant place; it is the immediate awakening to one's own immortal nature right here and now, bringing an end to all relative limitations.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The text concludes by celebrating the final return of the soul to its divine source. Guided by the true understanding of Brahman, the soul transcends material limitations and enters the eternal company of the Lord, experiencing supreme bliss and loving service forever.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva concludes that salvation is the ultimate release from the physical world, granted as a gift of grace to the soul who has realized its complete dependency on the Lord. The soul reaches the presence of Viṣṇu, where it remains eternally distinct from Him but filled to its perfect capacity with divine joy.
